note: Single source of truth for all mission timeline and crew schedule data. Enrichment detail+links live in data.js EVENT_DETAILS.

events.title: Launch Team Arrives to Stations
events.summary: Launch team personnel report to their consoles in the Launch Control Center at Kennedy Space Center.

events.title: Countdown Clock Begins
events.summary: The official launch countdown clock starts at L−49 hours 40 minutes.

events.title: Orion Spacecraft Powered Up
events.summary: The Orion crew module and European Service Module are powered on and begin system self-checks.

events.title: Core Stage Powered Up
events.summary: The SLS core stage avionics and flight computers are powered on for pre-flight testing.

events.title: Final Preparations of RS-25 Engines
events.summary: Technicians complete final inspections and preparations on the four RS-25 main engines.

events.title: ICPS Powered Up for Launch
events.summary: The Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage upper stage is powered on and its RL10B-2 engine systems are checked.

events.title: Non-Essential Personnel Leave Pad
events.summary: All non-essential workers are cleared from Launch Complex 39B as the countdown enters its critical phase.

events.title: Ground Launch Sequencer Activation
events.summary: The Ground Launch Sequencer (GLS) software is activated and begins monitoring vehicle health.

events.title: Built-in 2h 45m Hold Begins
events.summary: A planned 2-hour 45-minute hold in the countdown allows teams to catch up on procedures and resolve any open issues.

events.title: Go/No-Go for Tanking
events.summary: The Launch Director polls the team for permission to begin loading cryogenic propellants into the SLS core stage.

events.title: Core Stage LH₂ Slow Fill
events.summary: Loading of liquid hydrogen into the SLS core stage begins with a slow fill to chill down the tank and feed lines.

events.title: Resume T-Clock from T−8h 10m
events.summary: The T-clock resumes counting from T−8 hours 10 minutes, running in parallel with the L-clock.

events.title: Core Stage LOX Slow Fill
events.summary: Liquid oxygen loading begins into the SLS core stage, starting with slow fill to chill the tank.

events.title: Orion Comms Activated (RF to Mission Control)
events.summary: Orion’s RF communication link to Mission Control Houston is activated and verified.

events.title: Closeout Crew Assemble
events.summary: The closeout crew assembles at the pad to prepare for crew arrival and ingress into Orion.

events.title: Flight Crew Weather Brief
events.summary: The flight crew receives the final weather briefing from the 45th Weather Squadron at Patrick Space Force Base.

events.title: Built-in 1h 10m Hold Begins
events.summary: A planned 1-hour 10-minute hold provides final margin before crew transport to the pad.

events.title: Flight Crew Deployment to Pad
events.summary: The crew departs the Neil Armstrong Operations and Checkout Building for the 9-mile drive to Launch Complex 39B.

events.title: Flight Crew Board Orion (Ingress)
events.summary: The four crew members enter the Orion spacecraft via the Crew Access Arm and take their assigned seats.

events.title: Crew Module Hatch Preps and Closure
events.summary: The closeout crew prepares and closes the Orion crew module hatch, then performs leak checks.

events.title: Hatch Service Panel Install / Closeouts
events.summary: The exterior hatch service panel is installed and final closeout tasks are performed on the crew module.

events.title: Launch Abort System Hatch Closure
events.summary: The Launch Abort System (LAS) hatch is sealed, completing the closeout of the crew escape path.

events.title: Launch Director Brief
events.summary: Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson briefs the team on countdown status and any open items.

events.title: Closeout Crew Departs Pad
events.summary: The closeout crew leaves the mobile launcher and evacuates to the Launch Control Center, 5.6 km away.

events.title: Final NTD Briefing
events.summary: The NASA Test Director (NTD) delivers the final pre-launch briefing to all teams.

events.title: Built-in 30-Minute Hold
events.summary: The final planned hold in the countdown — 30 minutes of margin for last-minute issue resolution.

events.title: Transition to Orion–Earth Comm Loop
events.summary: Communications switch from ground-based loops to the direct Orion–Earth space communication link.

events.title: Launch Director Polls Team for Go
events.summary: The Launch Director polls all console positions for a final “Go/No-Go” decision to proceed with launch.

events.title: Flight Crew Visors Down
events.summary: The four crew members lower and lock their helmet visors, sealing their OCSS pressure suits for launch.

events.title: Terminal Countdown Begins (GLS)
events.summary: The Ground Launch Sequencer takes active control at T−10 minutes, beginning the automated terminal countdown.

events.title: Crew Access Arm Retract
events.summary: The 55-metre Crew Access Arm swings away from Orion and locks into launch position.

events.title: Core Stage Tank Pressurization / Orion to Internal Power
events.summary: Core stage propellant tanks are pressurised to flight levels and Orion switches to internal battery power.

events.title: LAS Capability Available
events.summary: The Launch Abort System is armed and ready to pull the crew module to safety if an abort is triggered.

events.title: Flight Termination System Armed
events.summary: The flight termination system is armed, giving Range Safety the ability to destroy the vehicle if it goes off course.

events.title: Core Stage APU Start
events.summary: The core stage Auxiliary Power Units start, providing hydraulic power for RS-25 engine gimballing.

events.title: Purge Sequence 4
events.summary: The fourth and final purge sequence flushes the engine compartment with inert gas to prevent hydrogen accumulation.

events.title: Booster to Internal Power
events.summary: The twin solid rocket boosters switch to internal battery power in preparation for ignition.

events.title: Core Stage to Internal Power
events.summary: The SLS core stage disconnects from ground power and switches to internal batteries.

events.title: ICPS LH₂ Terminate Replenish
events.summary: Liquid hydrogen replenishment to the ICPS upper stage is terminated and the fill line is secured.

events.title: Go for Automated Launch Sequencer
events.summary: Final “Go” is given and the automated launch sequencer takes full control for the final seconds.

events.title: Hydrogen Burn-Off Igniters
events.summary: Hydrogen burn-off igniters activate beneath the RS-25 engines to safely combust any free hydrogen before ignition.

events.title: RS-25 Engine Start Command
events.summary: The start command is sent to the four RS-25 engines, initiating the staggered ignition sequence.

events.title: RS-25 Engines Ignite
events.summary: All four RS-25 engines reach full thrust. The vehicle strains against the hold-down posts as systems are verified.

events.title: Booster Ignition — Liftoff!
events.summary: The twin solid rocket boosters ignite and the eight hold-down bolts are severed pyrotechnically. SLS lifts off from LC-39B.

events.title: Liftoff
events.summary: SLS lifts off from LC-39B at Kennedy Space Center. Nearly 40 MN of thrust pushes the crew toward orbit at ~8 km/s.

events.title: Max Q (Maximum Dynamic Pressure)
events.summary: The vehicle passes through the point of maximum aerodynamic stress at approximately T+60 seconds. Altitude ~14 km.

events.title: SRB Separation
events.summary: The twin solid rocket boosters separate after burning for ~126 seconds, at an altitude of ~45 km.

events.title: Service Module Fairing Jettison
events.summary: The protective fairing panels around the European Service Module are jettisoned, exposing the solar arrays and radiators.

events.title: Launch Abort System Jettison
events.summary: The Launch Abort System tower is jettisoned. It is no longer needed as the vehicle is above the altitude where it could safely pull the crew module away.

events.title: Core Stage MECO (Main Engine Cutoff)
events.summary: The four RS-25 engines shut down after ~8 minutes 24 seconds of powered flight. The core stage has done its job.

events.title: Core Stage Separation & ICPS Ignition
events.summary: Core stage separates. The Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage (ICPS) ignites to reach initial parking orbit.

events.title: ICPS Cutoff / Orbital Insertion
events.summary: ICPS completes its first burn. Orion reaches initial low Earth orbit at ~185 km (115 mi) altitude.

events.title: Solar Array Deployment
events.summary: The four solar array wings on the European Service Module unfurl to their full 19-metre span, generating 11.2 kW of electrical power.

events.title: Crew Checks Begin
events.summary: Crew begins post-insertion checks: verifying suit integrity, cabin pressure, communications, and reviewing vehicle systems displays.

events.title: ICPS Perigee Raise Burn 1
events.summary: ICPS fires at T+49 min to raise orbit from 185 km circular to a highly elliptical orbit with apogee ~70,400 km.

events.title: ICPS Perigee Raise Burn 2
events.summary: Second ICPS burn further raises the orbit into high Earth orbit for systems checkout.

events.title: Orion Systems Checkout Begins
events.summary: Crew begins comprehensive systems checkout: testing water dispenser, toilet, CO₂ removal, and reconfiguring the cabin for orbital operations.

events.title: Proximity Operations Demo — ICPS Separation
events.summary: Orion separates from the spent ICPS upper stage at MET 3h 24m. The crew practises manual flying toward and around it as a docking-target surrogate.

events.title: Orion Upper Stage Separation Burn
events.summary: Orion fires its ESM thrusters to increase distance from the spent ICPS, ending proximity operations.

events.title: ICPS Disposal Burn
events.summary: The spent ICPS performs a disposal burn to move into a safe heliocentric orbit, away from Orion’s path.

events.title: CubeSat Deployment Sequence
events.summary: Ten small CubeSat satellites deploy from the ICPS at one-minute intervals, beginning secondary science missions in deep space.

events.title: Crew Sleep Period 1
events.summary: Brief rest period after launch. Crew woken at MET ~12.5h for the perigee raise burn.

events.title: Orbit Geometry Burn
events.summary: Engine firing adjusts orbital geometry to set up the correct approach angle for the TLI burn on Flight Day 2.

events.title: Deep Space Network Emergency Comms Checkout
events.summary: First test of Orion’s emergency communication system via the Deep Space Network — verifying the link that will be critical during the lunar phase.

events.title: Crew Sleep Period 2
events.summary: After the perigee raise burn, crew returns to sleep until MET 20h. Crew requested wakeup music at MET 20:00.

events.title: Crew Exercise
events.summary: Crew rotates on the flywheel resistive exercise device before the TLI burn.

events.title: TLI Burn Preparation
events.summary: Koch prepares Orion’s orbital manoeuvring system engine for the critical translunar injection burn.

events.title: ⭐ Trans-Lunar Injection (TLI) Burn
events.summary: THE critical burn — ESM main engine fires for ~6 min 5 s, adding ~1,450 km/h to escape Earth orbit and set course for the Moon.

events.title: Post-TLI Crew Acclimation
events.summary: Crew verifies spacecraft health after TLI and begins adjusting to the outbound cruise phase. Lighter duties as Earth recedes.

events.title: First Space-to-Ground Video Communication
events.summary: The crew conducts their first live video session with Mission Control Houston, sharing the view of Earth receding behind them.

events.title: Outbound Trajectory Correction Burn 1
events.summary: First of three outbound mid-course corrections, fine-tuning Orion’s path to the Moon.

events.title: CPR Demonstration in Microgravity
events.summary: Glover, Koch, and Hansen demonstrate CPR procedures adapted for microgravity — a critical emergency skill for deep-space missions.

events.title: Medical Kit Checkout
events.summary: Wiseman and Glover test the onboard medical kit: thermometer, blood pressure monitor, stethoscope, otoscope, and medications.

events.title: Emergency Comms Test on Deep Space Network
events.summary: Koch tests Orion’s emergency communication modes through the Deep Space Network at increasing distance from Earth.

events.title: Emergency Procedure Practice — Cabin Depress & Fire
events.summary: Crew runs through emergency procedures for cabin depressurisation and fire — two of the most dangerous scenarios in deep space where rescue is impossible.

events.title: Outbound Science Experiments
events.summary: The crew conducts a suite of biomedical and technology experiments during the outbound transit — the first human-tended experiments beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo.

events.title: Lunar Observation Rehearsal
events.summary: The crew rehearses the choreography for Flight Day 6’s lunar flyby observations — camera positions, attitude manoeuvres, and target sequences.

events.title: GPS & TDRS Range Limit — Beyond Earth’s Navigation Grid
events.summary: Orion passes beyond the effective range of GPS satellites and the TDRS relay network — from here, only NASA’s Deep Space Network can reach the crew.

events.title: Emergency Procedure Practice — Rapid Abort Scenarios
events.summary: Second emergency drill session: the crew practises abort scenarios including an emergency return trajectory burn and contingency power-down procedures.

events.title: Outbound Trajectory Correction Burn 2
events.summary: Second mid-course correction refines Orion’s lunar approach path.

events.title: Lunar Geography Target Study
events.summary: Each crew member reviews their assigned geographic targets for lunar flyby imaging — specific craters, maria, and the South Pole–Aitken Basin.

events.title: Celestial Photography Session
events.summary: Crew photographs celestial bodies from Orion’s windows — a 20-minute dedicated photography session capturing views impossible from Earth.

events.title: Spacesuit Pressure Tests
events.summary: Crew tests rapid donning and pressurisation of the Orion Crew Survival System (OCSS) suits — practising emergency scenarios.

events.title: Suit Eat/Drink Port Test
events.summary: Crew tests eating and drinking through the helmet port while suited — validating the ability to sustain crew during extended suited operations.

events.title: Outbound Trajectory Correction Burn 3
events.summary: Final outbound mid-course correction before the lunar flyby — last chance to fine-tune the approach trajectory.

events.title: ⭐ Enter Lunar Sphere of Influence
events.summary: Orion crosses into the Moon’s gravitational sphere of influence at ~66,200 km from the lunar centre — the Moon’s gravity is now the dominant force acting on the spacecraft.

events.title: Approach to Moon
events.summary: Orion begins its close approach to the Moon. The crew prepares cameras and observation equipment for the historic flyby.

events.title: ⭐ Closest Approach — ~8,900 km Above Lunar Surface
events.summary: The emotional and navigational heart of the mission — Orion passes ~8,900 km (~5,530 mi) above the Moon, closer than any crewed spacecraft since Apollo.

events.title: Lunar Surface Observation & Photography
events.summary: Intensive photography and scientific observation of the lunar surface during the close-approach window.

events.title: ⭐ Far Side of Moon — Communications Blackout (30–50 min)
events.summary: Orion passes behind the Moon. All radio contact with Earth is lost for 30–50 minutes — the crew is completely on their own, farther from contact with home than any humans in history.

events.title: Far-Side Photography Session
events.summary: During the communications blackout, the crew turns all cameras toward the Moon’s far side — a landscape no human eye has seen directly since Apollo 17.

events.title: Signal Reacquired
events.summary: Orion emerges from behind the Moon and re-establishes communication with Earth. Mission Control confirms spacecraft health.

events.title: ⭐ Maximum Distance from Earth — ~4,600 mi Beyond the Far Side
events.summary: Orion reaches its maximum distance from Earth at approximately 405,500 km — surpassing Apollo 13’s record of 400,171 km by over 5,300 km, and passing roughly 4,600 miles beyond the Moon’s far side.

events.title: Post-Flyby Science Observations
events.summary: Continued photography and observations as Orion departs the Moon — capturing the receding lunar landscape and "Earthrise" views.

events.title: Exit Lunar Sphere of Influence
events.summary: Orion exits the Moon’s gravitational sphere of influence — Earth’s gravity is once again the dominant force. The homeward journey accelerates from here.

events.title: Crew Off-Duty Day — Rest & Personal Time
events.summary: Largely off-duty day for the crew to rest and recover after the intense lunar flyby. Minimal scheduled activities.

events.title: Return Trajectory Correction Burn 1
events.summary: First of three return course-correction burns to adjust Orion’s path toward the re-entry corridor.

events.title: Radiation Shelter Demonstration
events.summary: Crew builds a protective radiation shelter using Orion’s supplies and equipment — demonstrating shielding from solar particle events.

events.title: Manual Piloting Demo — 6-DOF vs 3-DOF
events.summary: Crew tests Orion’s manual flight control modes: target centering, tail-to-Sun attitude, and comparing 6-DOF vs 3-DOF handling.

events.title: Return Trajectory Correction Burn 2
events.summary: Second return course correction keeps Orion precisely on target for the Pacific Ocean splashdown zone.

events.title: Re-Entry Procedure Review
events.summary: Crew studies re-entry procedures and coordinates with the flight control team on the timeline for atmospheric entry.

events.title: Equipment Stow Begins
events.summary: Crew begins securing loose equipment and reconfiguring the cabin for the high-g re-entry environment.

events.title: Final Stow & Cabin Prep
events.summary: Final cabin preparations for re-entry — all equipment secured, seats in landing position, cabin in launch configuration.

events.title: Crew Dons Spacesuits for Re-Entry
events.summary: All four crew members don their OCSS pressure suits, close visors, and strap into their seats for atmospheric entry.

events.title: Service Module Separation
events.summary: The European Service Module separates from the crew module via pyrotechnic bolts. Only the capsule continues to splashdown.

events.title: ⭐ Entry Interface — Hitting Atmosphere at 40,000 km/h
events.summary: Orion enters Earth’s atmosphere at 120 km altitude, travelling at ~40,000 km/h (25,000 mph). Heat shield faces ~2,760 °C (5,000 °F).

events.title: Re-Entry Communications Blackout
events.summary: Ionised plasma around the capsule blocks all radio signals for approximately 5 minutes during peak heating.

events.title: Drogue Parachute Deploy
events.summary: At ~11,000 m (36,000 ft), two drogue parachutes deploy to stabilise and slow the capsule from ~480 km/h to ~200 km/h.

events.title: Main Parachute Deploy
events.summary: Three 35 m (116 ft) main parachutes open in stages, slowing Orion to ~27 km/h (17 mph) for splashdown.

events.title: ⭐ Splashdown in Pacific Ocean
events.summary: Orion splashes down in the Pacific Ocean ~370 km off San Diego, California. The Artemis II mission is complete.

events.title: Crew Recovery by USS Portland
events.summary: Navy divers stabilise the capsule. Crew exits and is helicopter-lifted to the USS Portland (LPD-27) for medical evaluation.

events.title: AVATAR Science Payload Checkout
events.summary: Crew checks out the AVATAR scientific instrument package for deep space science observations.

events.title: 🌑 Solar Eclipse from Lunar Orbit
events.summary: The Sun passes behind the Moon from Orion's perspective. Nearly one hour of eclipse — crew observes the solar corona, meteoroid impacts on the dark surface, and lofted lunar dust.

crewSchedule.label: Launch & ascent
crewSchedule.desc: SLS liftoff, main-engine cutoff ~8 min after launch.

crewSchedule.label: Orbital insertion burns
crewSchedule.desc: ICPS perigee-raise burn (~49 min), then high-Earth orbit insertion (~1 hr).

crewSchedule.label: Post-insertion checks
crewSchedule.desc: Remove launch suits, reconfigure cabin for living and work.

crewSchedule.label: Proximity operations demo
crewSchedule.desc: ICPS becomes docking target; crew practices manual flying toward and around it.

crewSchedule.label: Orion systems checkout
crewSchedule.desc: Test potable water dispenser, toilet, CO₂ removal system.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: First meal in orbit — test food rehydration system.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Brief rest period (~4 hours) after first day in space.

crewSchedule.label: Orbital manoeuvre & DSN checkout
crewSchedule.desc: Engine firing for correct TLI orbital geometry; emergency comms test on Deep Space Network.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: After perigee raise burn, crew returns to sleep for ~4.5 hours before first full day in space.

crewSchedule.label: Wake & meal
crewSchedule.desc: Morning routine and breakfast.

crewSchedule.label: TLI burn conference — GO for TLI
crewSchedule.desc: Mission management team gave go for translunar injection.

crewSchedule.label: Crew exercise
crewSchedule.desc: Flywheel resistive exercise. Crew rotates on the single device.

crewSchedule.label: TLI burn preparation
crewSchedule.desc: Final systems checkout, crew dons pressure suits.

crewSchedule.label: Translunar injection burn
crewSchedule.desc: ESM main engine fires for ~6 minutes. Commits Orion to the Moon.

crewSchedule.label: Post-TLI checks
crewSchedule.desc: Systems verification. Earth shadow transit.

crewSchedule.label: Video downlink & crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: First video call home after TLI.

crewSchedule.label: Personal time
crewSchedule.desc: Off-duty after a historic day.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal & pre-sleep
crewSchedule.desc: Evening meal before transit sleep.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Full rest period after TLI day.

crewSchedule.label: Wake & meal
crewSchedule.desc: Morning routine and breakfast.

crewSchedule.label: Trajectory correction burn #1 prep
crewSchedule.desc: Hansen prepares for first of three outbound course-correction burns.

crewSchedule.label: Outbound trajectory correction burn #1
crewSchedule.desc: First mid-course correction ensures Orion stays on target for the Moon.

crewSchedule.label: CPR & medical kit demo
crewSchedule.desc: Glover, Koch, and Hansen demonstrate CPR procedures in microgravity; Wiseman and Glover check out medical kit.

crewSchedule.label: DSN emergency comms test
crewSchedule.desc: Koch tests Orion's emergency communications system on the Deep Space Network.

crewSchedule.label: Lunar flyby rehearsal
crewSchedule.desc: Entire crew rehearses choreography for Day 6 lunar flyby scientific observations.

crewSchedule.label: Exercise & personal time
crewSchedule.desc: Crew exercise and downtime.

crewSchedule.label: Video downlink
crewSchedule.desc: Scheduled space-to-ground video session.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Full rest period.

crewSchedule.label: Wake & meal
crewSchedule.desc: Morning routine and breakfast.

crewSchedule.label: Trajectory correction burn #2 prep
crewSchedule.desc: Preparation for second outbound course correction.

crewSchedule.label: Outbound trajectory correction burn #2
crewSchedule.desc: Second mid-course correction refines lunar approach path.

crewSchedule.label: Lunar target study
crewSchedule.desc: Each astronaut reviews geographic targets for Day 6 imagery — exact targets based on launch day/time.

crewSchedule.label: Celestial photography
crewSchedule.desc: Crew photographs celestial bodies from Orion's windows.

crewSchedule.label: Exercise
crewSchedule.desc: Flywheel workout session.

crewSchedule.label: Video downlink & personal time
crewSchedule.desc: Video call with ground and free time.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: Evening meal.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Full rest period.

crewSchedule.label: Wake & meal
crewSchedule.desc: Morning routine and breakfast.

crewSchedule.label: Spacesuit pressure testing
crewSchedule.desc: Test donning suits quickly, pressurizing them, installing seats, eating/drinking through helmet port.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal & rest
crewSchedule.desc: Midday break.

crewSchedule.label: Trajectory correction burn #3 prep
crewSchedule.desc: Preparation for final outbound course correction before lunar flyby.

crewSchedule.label: Outbound trajectory correction burn #3
crewSchedule.desc: Final outbound mid-course correction. Orion enters the lunar sphere of influence today.

crewSchedule.label: Exercise
crewSchedule.desc: Flywheel workout session.

crewSchedule.label: Video downlink & personal time
crewSchedule.desc: Video call with ground and free time.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: Evening meal.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Full rest period before lunar flyby day.

crewSchedule.label: Wake early — lunar flyby day
crewSchedule.desc: Early wake-up for the historic lunar flyby.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal & flyby prep
crewSchedule.desc: Breakfast and final preparation for close lunar approach.

crewSchedule.label: Lunar flyby — photography & observations
crewSchedule.desc: Closest approach: 4,000–6,000 miles from lunar surface. Moon appears basketball-sized at arm's length. Crew photographs and records observations.

crewSchedule.label: Solar eclipse observation
crewSchedule.desc: Sun hidden behind Moon. Crew observes corona, meteoroid flashes, lunar dust.

crewSchedule.label: Far-side transit — loss of signal
crewSchedule.desc: Orion passes behind the Moon. Communications blackout lasting 30–50 minutes.

crewSchedule.label: Post-flyby science & observation
crewSchedule.desc: Continued photography and recordings after reacquiring signal. First humans to see parts of the lunar far side with their own eyes.

crewSchedule.label: Exercise & crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: Post-flyby meal and exercise.

crewSchedule.label: Video downlink
crewSchedule.desc: Share lunar flyby experience with Mission Control and the world.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Rest period after the historic flyby.

crewSchedule.label: Wake & meal
crewSchedule.desc: Morning routine. Orion exits the lunar sphere of influence today.

crewSchedule.label: Crew lunar debrief
crewSchedule.desc: Ground scientists speak with crew while the lunar flyby experience is fresh.

crewSchedule.label: Return trajectory correction burn #1
crewSchedule.desc: First of three return course corrections to adjust path home.

crewSchedule.label: Exercise
crewSchedule.desc: Flywheel workout session.

crewSchedule.label: Off-duty time
crewSchedule.desc: Largely off-duty day for rest before final return phase tasks.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: Evening meal.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Extended rest period.

crewSchedule.label: Wake & meal
crewSchedule.desc: Morning routine and breakfast.

crewSchedule.label: Radiation shelter demonstration
crewSchedule.desc: Crew builds a protective shelter using Orion supplies — demonstrating shielding from solar particle events.

crewSchedule.label: Manual piloting demonstration
crewSchedule.desc: Test Orion manual control: target centering, tail-to-Sun attitude, 6-DOF vs 3-DOF manoeuvres.

crewSchedule.label: Exercise
crewSchedule.desc: Flywheel workout session.

crewSchedule.label: Video downlink & crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: Video call with ground and evening meal.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Full rest period.

crewSchedule.label: Wake & meal
crewSchedule.desc: Morning routine and breakfast.

crewSchedule.label: Re-entry & splashdown procedure review
crewSchedule.desc: Crew studies re-entry procedures and talks with flight control team.

crewSchedule.label: Return trajectory correction burn #2
crewSchedule.desc: Course correction to keep Orion on target for splashdown zone.

crewSchedule.label: Waste system & garment testing
crewSchedule.desc: Test backup waste collection systems; fit-check orthostatic intolerance compression garments.

crewSchedule.label: Exercise & personal time
crewSchedule.desc: Final workout session and personal time.

crewSchedule.label: Crew meal
crewSchedule.desc: Evening meal — last full evening in space.

crewSchedule.label: Sleep period
crewSchedule.desc: Final rest period before re-entry day.

crewSchedule.label: Wake early — re-entry day
crewSchedule.desc: Early wake-up for the final day of the mission.

crewSchedule.label: Final stow & cabin prep
crewSchedule.desc: Return cabin to launch configuration: stow equipment, install seats.

crewSchedule.label: Don spacesuits for re-entry
crewSchedule.desc: Crew dons OCSS pressure suits, closes visors, and straps in for atmospheric entry.

crewSchedule.label: Service module separation
crewSchedule.desc: Crew module separates from European Service Module prior to atmospheric entry.

crewSchedule.label: Atmospheric re-entry
crewSchedule.desc: Heat shield faces temperatures up to ~2,760 °C (5,000 °F) during re-entry at ~40,000 km/h.

crewSchedule.label: Parachute descent
crewSchedule.desc: Drogue chutes slow to ~494 km/h, then three main chutes deploy — final descent at ~27 km/h.

crewSchedule.label: Splashdown & recovery
crewSchedule.desc: Splashdown in the Pacific Ocean. NASA and U.S. Navy recovery teams retrieve crew and capsule.
